_meta: DRAFT — AUTO-GENERATED, REQUIRES HUMAN REVIEW BEFORE USE. This gold set was generated from the GymStart corpus (corpus/app and corpus/fitness). Questions are deliberately paraphrased away from the source wording so lexical overlap alone won't retrieve the right chunk, and some are worded to plausibly lure the wrong overlapping document (retrieval-precision traps). Reference answers should be verified against the cited source_doc/source_section. Unanswerable items intentionally describe features/numbers the corpus does NOT support; their reference_answer is a correct refusal. Review for accuracy, difficulty calibration, and coverage before relying on this set.

question: If I want GymStart to stop suggesting me a machine, is there a way to just take it out of the current workout without deleting anything?
reference_answer: Yes. On the Today screen you can swipe a machine left or right to remove it from today's session. Removing the last machine leaves the list empty rather than refilling it.
source_section: The machine list is the editor
notes: Paraphrased; 'swipe to remove' never stated in the question.

question: When I pick a different exercise to replace one in my lineup, does it have to come from the same rotation half?
reference_answer: No. Swapping is a free choice — you can swap in any available machine, including one from the other group (A or B). The swap keeps the machine's position so the rest of the session is undisturbed.
source_section: The machine list is the editor
notes: Tests understanding that swaps cross groups.

question: How much time does the app give me to catch my breath between sets, and can I cut it short?
reference_answer: A 60-second rest timer starts automatically between sets (never after the last set), and you can tap 'Skip rest' at any time.
notes: Specific number (60s) is stated in the doc.

question: After a workout, how many times does the app check in with me about how it went?
reference_answer: Just once per machine — the single 'How did that feel?' question with three choices (Easy, Just right, Hard). It's the only feedback the app asks for.
notes: Paraphrase of 'the only question we ask'.

question: What does tapping the '3' chip actually do to a day I'd already customized by hand?
reference_answer: Tapping a count chip (2/3/4) clears any hand-picked machines for today first, then builds a fresh suggested session of that size. This is intentional so the chip isn't overridden by an earlier explicit pick.
source_section: Choosing how many machines: the Suggest chips
notes: Tests the clear-hand-picks behavior.

question: Does GymStart make me register with an email before I can use it?
reference_answer: No. GymStart has no accounts, no email, and no password. You go straight from the welcome screen into onboarding, and everything stays on your device.
notes: Overlaps with getting-started and faq; best source is accounts-and-data.

question: I'm switching phones. How do I bring my history and progress across?
reference_answer: Use Settings → Backup → 'Copy my data' to export everything as text, save it somewhere (a note or email to yourself), then use 'Restore from a backup' on the new phone. Restoring replaces everything on the new phone. Machine photos and simulated sessions do not travel.
source_section: Moving to a new phone: backup and restore
notes: Overlaps with settings-reference and faq.

question: How far apart does the app space out its lighter recovery weeks, and how much does it drop the weight?
reference_answer: About every seven sessions GymStart nudges a recovery week, showing that session's weights roughly 20% lighter (rounded to the nearest 5 lb). Your stored progression isn't changed — it's a display adjustment for that session only.
notes: Two specific numbers: ~7 sessions, ~20%.

question: How does the 'what is this machine' helper work — do I point my phone at it?
reference_answer: No camera is used. The Machine Finder (search icon on the Today screen) asks two or three plain-language questions and narrows down to the exact machine, then shows its photo, safety tip, and why it works. A camera version would need a vision model; the guided flow works offline.
source_section: Why it's a guided flow and not a camera
notes: Trap: question implies a camera, which the app does NOT have.

question: My gym has an exercise machine that isn't in the app's list. Can I put it in myself?
reference_answer: Yes. Go to Settings → My machines → Add a machine. You give it a name, what it works, an A/B day, and a starting weight (photo and specific muscles optional). Sets and reps default to 3 × 8–12. Custom machines are always available to you.
notes: Default 3x8-12 stated in doc.

question: What are the highest and lowest number of sets I'm allowed to set for a single machine?
reference_answer: Sets per machine can be set between 1 and 6. (Reps per machine are bounded 5–30.)
notes: Specific bounds; also in state/app_state constants.

question: If a machine keeps feeling too tough, will GymStart automatically drop the weight for me?
reference_answer: No. The app never lowers weight automatically. After two 'Hard' sessions in a row it suggests going lighter (on the machine screen and finish screen), but you make the change yourself with the − stepper.
source_section: The app never lowers weight for you
notes: Overlaps with aches-and-safety-features and faq.

question: If I quit a workout partway through, do I keep the gains on the machines I already did?
reference_answer: Yes. When you end a session early, progress on machines you already answered 'How did that feel?' for is kept (saved as it happens). Only the unfinished session isn't written into your history log.
notes: Overlaps with faq.

question: What's the deal with turning on that date-picking preview feature, and can I wipe the fake sessions afterward?
reference_answer: That's simulation mode (Settings → Simulation, off by default). When on, starting a session first asks you to pick a date, and the session is logged as a 'what-if' record. You can wipe all simulated data via 'Delete simulated data' in Settings; your real sessions stay, but weight changes those simulated sessions caused are not rewound.
notes: Spans setup + cleanup within one doc; also touched in settings-reference.

question: How does the app estimate which future days it thinks I'll show up to train?
reference_answer: It computes your typical cadence as the median gap between your training days (clamped to 1–7 days) once you have at least two training days, then projects upcoming days by spacing forward from your last session by that cadence. These are gentle guesses, not a committed schedule.
source_section: How the calendar figures out your rhythm
notes: Requires the cadence/projection detail.

question: When the app decides on its own to bump a machine's weight up, does that heavier number get carried into the next recovery week, or will that week still look lighter?
reference_answer: Weight increases automatically after four Easy/Just-right sessions accumulate on a machine (raising the stored working weight). Separately, on a recovery week (about every seven sessions) that stored weight is shown ~20% lighter for that session only, without changing the stored value. So the recovery week still displays lighter even after an auto-increase — the increase is in the stored weight, the deload is a temporary display drop.
source_section: How weight goes up; Recovery weeks (deload)
notes: MULTI-HOP: combines auto-increase rule with deload display behavior across two docs.

question: I flagged bad knees at setup and then added my own leg machine. Will the app start that custom machine lighter the way it does for the built-in knee machines?
reference_answer: Not automatically. Built-in machines that load a flagged ache start one increment lighter and show a form cue. But a custom machine you add uses the starting weight you enter yourself and doesn't carry ache-sensitivity, so the ache-based lighter start and cues apply to bundled machines, not to your custom one.
source_section: What flagging an ache does; Adding a machine
notes: MULTI-HOP: ache handling (bundled) vs custom-machine setup (user sets weight, no sensitiveTo).

question: If I copy my data to a new phone, will my what-if preview sessions and my custom machine photos show up there too?
reference_answer: No to both. Backups never include simulated (what-if) sessions, and machine photos are stored as file paths rather than image data, so a restored custom machine shows the placeholder until you set a photo again.
source_section: Moving to a new phone: backup and restore; What it does and doesn't affect
notes: MULTI-HOP: backup-excludes-simulated + photos-as-paths; also in custom-machines doc.

question: Does the machine identifier tool ever surface a machine that I couldn't actually get into one of my sessions?
reference_answer: Yes. The Machine Finder can identify any machine in its tree, and if the result isn't in your gym's usual line-up it shows a heads-up that it won't appear in your sessions — because generated sessions and the swap sheet only draw from your available pool. To make it available, add it to your pool in Settings → Machines I have (or add it as a custom machine).
source_section: How it works; How the pool affects sessions
notes: MULTI-HOP: finder can name any machine, but availability pool governs sessions.

question: I set a custom rep range for a machine in settings and my goal is 'get stronger'. Which range will I see on that machine during a session?
reference_answer: Your saved custom rep range wins. The goal ('get stronger') only shifts the default rep range; once you've set your own sets/reps for a machine in Settings → Sets & reps, that saved range is used and the app never changes it — the goal-based shift no longer applies to that machine.
source_section: Reps are a range, and they're yours; Adjustable settings
notes: MULTI-HOP: precedence of user-set reps over goal-shifted default.

question: For someone lifting weights regularly, about how much protein per kilo of bodyweight is usually recommended?
reference_answer: Roughly 1.6 to 2.2 grams of protein per kilogram of bodyweight per day (about 0.7–1.0 g per pound) is commonly supported for adults doing regular resistance training. This is general information, not medical advice.
notes: Core protein number.

question: Why can't I just keep doing the exact same workout forever if it's working?
reference_answer: Because your body adapts to a fixed demand and stops changing — you get efficient at that workout but stop getting stronger. Progressive overload means gradually increasing the challenge (weight, reps, sets, range, tempo) so muscles keep having a reason to adapt.
notes: Paraphrased.

question: Roughly how many hours of sleep should an adult aim for to recover well from training?
reference_answer: About 7–9 hours per night for most adults. Sleep is the most important recovery tool, and much of the body's tissue repair happens during it.
notes: Specific 7-9 hours.

question: Do I actually need a sports drink for a normal hour-long gym session, or is water fine?
reference_answer: For most everyday gym sessions under about an hour, plain water is enough. Electrolyte or carbohydrate drinks are mainly useful for prolonged (roughly 60–90+ minutes), intense, or very sweaty exercise, or hot conditions.
source_section: Do you need sports drinks or electrolytes?
notes: Paraphrased.

question: Is it better to stretch and hold poses before lifting, or should I move around instead?
reference_answer: Before lifting, favor a general warm-up (a few minutes of easy cardio), some dynamic (moving) stretches, and a light ramp-up set. Long static (held) stretches are best saved for after training, since holding them right before heavy lifting can briefly reduce power output.
notes: Static vs dynamic timing.

question: Both weights and running are on the table — do the official activity guidelines say to pick one?
reference_answer: No. General guidance for adults recommends both: at least ~150 minutes/week of moderate aerobic activity (or 75 vigorous) plus muscle-strengthening on 2 or more days a week. Weight the balance toward your main goal, but some of each is the sweet spot.
notes: 150 min / 2 days figures.

question: As a total newbie, what rep count should most of my sets land in?
reference_answer: For most beginners, about 8–15 reps per set on most exercises is the sweet spot — it builds muscle effectively, is forgiving on form, and is gentle on the joints.
notes: Could lure workout-programming-basics too (overlap).

question: How many days a week should a beginner realistically lift?
reference_answer: Two to three full-body sessions a week is a well-supported, sustainable starting point — enough to progress, hitting each muscle group about twice weekly, and forgiving if you miss a day.
notes: Overlaps with workout-programming-basics; how-often-to-train is best source. Q&A doc so section left general.

question: What's the difference between being sore and being injured after a workout?
reference_answer: General muscle soreness a day or two later (DOMS) is normal, especially with new or harder training, and fades in a few days. Sharp, pinching, or joint pain during a movement is different — it's a signal to stop, reduce range or load, and get it checked if it persists.
notes: Also appears in recovery-and-sleep (DOMS) — overlap; form-cues has the soreness-vs-pain Q.

question: What's the most common thing people get wrong when they first start training?
reference_answer: Doing too much, too soon — long sessions, too many exercises, or too much weight early — which causes excessive soreness, burnout, or injury. The fix is to start with short, manageable sessions and let volume and intensity build up over weeks.
notes: Paraphrased.

question: Which muscles handle pushing versus pulling, and why does it matter for posture?
reference_answer: Pushing movements work the chest, front/side shoulders, and triceps; pulling movements work the back (lats, rhomboids), rear shoulders, and biceps. Desk life tends to over-develop the front and weaken the back, so emphasizing pulling and rear-shoulder work helps posture.
notes: Spans push/pull + posture note in one doc.

question: Is it too late to start lifting weights in my late fifties?
reference_answer: No — muscle and strength respond to training at every age. Progress is a bit slower and recovery takes longer, but adults in their 40s–60s+ consistently gain strength and muscle, which counters age-related muscle and bone loss and supports balance and everyday function. Get medical clearance before starting.
notes: Notes the professional-consult framing.

question: If I'm trying to lose fat, how big should my calorie cut be?
reference_answer: A modest deficit — often around 300–500 calories per day below maintenance — tends to be sustainable and preserves more muscle than aggressive dieting. Keep protein high and keep lifting. Aiming for roughly 0.5–1% of bodyweight change per week is a common guideline. General info, not medical advice.
notes: Specific deficit range in the expanded section.

question: How do I tell if the weight I picked is too heavy for me?
reference_answer: If you can't complete your reps with the same clean technique you used on the first rep, or you have to swing or cheat the weight up, it's too heavy — drop the load and keep form honest.
notes: Overlaps with gym-beginner-getting-started; form-cues has the direct Q&A.

question: What's the deal with 'double progression' — climbing reps before adding weight?
reference_answer: Double progression means training within a rep range: pick a weight you can do for the low end, work up to the top of the range over sessions, then add a small amount of weight and start again from the low end. It's a simple, reliable way to progress.
notes: Also described in rep-ranges-and-goals (overlap).

question: How should I be breathing during a lift?
reference_answer: A simple rule: exhale during the harder (lifting) phase and inhale during the easier (lowering) phase. The main thing is not to hold your breath and strain for long periods — just keep breathing.
notes: Q&A doc.

reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart is described as local-only with no accounts and nothing sent anywhere; no social-sharing feature is documented.
notes: No sharing feature exists in code or docs.

reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart does not track or display heart rate or heart-rate zones.
notes: No heart-rate feature; lures the strength-vs-cardio/warm-up docs.

reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. No wearable or device integration is described; the app stores everything locally and has no such connectivity.

reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart does not estimate or display calories burned.

reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart's sessions are built around gym machines; there's no documented cardio/running/cycling logging feature.
notes: Trap: strength-vs-cardio doc exists but app has no cardio logging.

question: How do I set a custom rest-timer length instead of the default in GymStart?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. The rest timer is described as 60 seconds and skippable, but no setting to change its length is documented.
notes: Rest timer exists (60s) but is not configurable — specific number/setting never stated.

question: What weight increment does GymStart use for someone who is 47 and 'somewhat active'?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't state this exact case. The docs say increments are 10 lb only for 'pretty active' users under 45, and 5 lb otherwise (including 'mostly sitting' or age 50+); the specific 47-year-old 'somewhat active' combination isn't spelled out as a stated value, though it would fall in the 5 lb 'otherwise' bucket. Treat as not explicitly covered / verify.
notes: REVIEWER NOTE: borderline. The rule implies 5 lb, but the exact case isn't stated verbatim. Included as an 'over-specific number' trap; a reviewer may reclassify as answerable-hard against how-progression-works.md → 'How big is one increment?'.

question: Does GymStart have a barcode scanner for logging the food I eat?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart is a training app with no food-logging or barcode-scanning feature described.
notes: Lures nutrition doc; no food logging in app.

question: How many rest days per week does GymStart schedule for me automatically?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. GymStart doesn't impose a training schedule or auto-schedule rest days; it shows gentle rest/reminder notes and projects likely upcoming days from your own rhythm, but never assigns a fixed number of rest days.
notes: Trap: recovery/how-often docs discuss rest, but the app doesn't schedule rest days.

question: What specific creatine dosage should I take with GymStart's program?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. The corpus contains no guidance on creatine or supplement dosing.
notes: No supplement-dosing content (protein doc mentions powder generally, not creatine dosing).

question: How do I export my progress charts as a PDF report?
reference_answer: The provided documents don't cover this. The only export described is the text backup for moving data between phones; there's no PDF or chart-export feature.
notes: Trap: backup export exists but is text-only, not PDF/report.
